title: Easton's Bible Dictionary
description: Illustrated Bible dictionary, third edition, published by Thomas Nelson, 1897. 3,964 entries.

entries.definition: The eldest son of Amram and Jochebed, a daughter of Levi. He was born in Egypt three years before his brother Moses, and a number of years after his sister Miriam. He was the high priest of the Jews, and first in the line of that office.
Destruction, the Hebrew name (equivalent to the Greek Apollyon, i.e., destroyer) of 'the angel of the bottomless pit'.

This Syriac or Chaldee word is found three times in the New Testament - Mark 14:36; Romans 8:15; Galatians 4:6 - in each case followed by its Greek equivalent, which is adopted in each case by the English versions.
(Hebrew: Hebel, vanity). The second son of Adam and Eve, murdered by his brother Cain. He was a keeper of sheep, while Cain was a tiller of the ground.
Father of abundance, or my father excels, the son of Ahimelech the high priest. He was the tenth high priest, and the fourth in descent from Eli.
Father of the king. (1) The name of several Philistine kings. (2) The son of Gideon, who made himself king of Shechem.
Father of a multitude, son of Terah, named (Genesis 11:27) before his older brothers Nahor and Haran, because he was the heir of the promises.
Exalted father. Abraham's original name.
Father of peace. The third son of David, and his favorite. His mother was Maacah, daughter of Talmai, king of Geshur.
The Greek word for bottomless pit, or depth, used to denote the abyss of hell.
Troubler. An Israelite of the tribe of Judah, who, when Jericho was taken, took of the accursed things.
Red, the first man, and the father of the human race. Created on the sixth day of creation, in the image of God.
My Lord is Jehovah. The fourth son of David.
Conjugal infidelity. An adulterer was a man who had illicit intercourse with a married or a betrothed woman.
(Gr. parakletos), one who pleads another's cause, who helps another by defending or comforting him. It is a name given by Christ three times to the Holy Ghost.
Flame, the name of the king of the Amalekites.
Love feasts. Meals shared among early Christians, associated with the Lord's Supper.
Father's brother. The name of two kings of Israel. The second and more famous was the son of Omri.
The Greek form of the Hebrew word rendered 'Artaxerxes' (Ezra). The same as Achshsharshah of Esther and Daniel.
Possessor. The name of several Old Testament persons, including a king of Judah.
Whom Jehovah holds. The name of two kings - one of Israel and one of Judah.
A structure on which sacrifices are offered. The first altar we read of was built by Noah on leaving the ark.
A tribe descended from Esau's grandson Amalek. They inhabited the desert between the Dead Sea and the Red Sea.
The descendants of Ben-ammi, Lot's son by his younger daughter. Their territory was east of the Jordan.
Burden. One of the twelve minor prophets. He was a herdsman of Tekoa, a town in Judah south of Jerusalem.
Jehovah has been gracious. The name of three men in the New Testament.
Manliness, the first of the apostles. He was a fisherman of Bethsaida.
Messenger. A word signifying, both in the Hebrew and Greek, a 'messenger,' and hence employed to denote any agent God sends forth to disclose his will.
The act of pouring oil on a person or thing. It was used in the consecration of priests, prophets, and kings.
Against Christ. Anyone who denies that Jesus is the Christ, or denies the Father and the Son.
The name of two cities: (1) Antioch of Syria, where believers were first called Christians. (2) Antioch of Pisidia.
The Greek name of the Book of Revelation. The uncovering or unveiling.
Hidden, spurious. Books not considered canonical by Protestants but included in the Septuagint and Vulgate.
A Jew from Alexandria, mighty in the Scriptures, instructed in the way of the Lord.
One sent forth with a special commission. The twelve disciples chosen by Jesus.
Sacred land or high land. The mountains where Noah's ark rested after the flood.
The sacred chest containing the tables of the Law, Aaron's rod, and a pot of manna.
The vessel built by Noah for the preservation of himself and family and animals during the flood.
The mount of Megiddo. The scene of the final conflict between Christ and Antichrist.
The Greek form of the name of several Persian kings.
Healer. A king of Judah who did what was right in the eyes of the Lord.
The visible ascent of our Lord from earth to heaven forty days after his resurrection.
Happy. The eighth son of Jacob, by Zilpah, Leah's handmaid.
The chief female divinity of the Phoenicians, a moon-goddess. Called Ishtar by Assyrians and Astarte by Greeks.
A country on the Tigris, the ancient capital of which was Nineveh.
This word does not occur in the Authorized Version of the New Testament except in Romans 5:11, where it properly signifies reconciliation.
The cognomen given to Octavius, the adopted son of Julius Caesar, and first emperor of Rome.
(Leviticus 16:8, 10, 26). The Scapegoat, or the goat that was sent away.
Lord, master. A title of honor given to gods and men of rank.
Lord. The supreme male divinity of the Phoenician and Canaanitish nations.
Confusion. The name given to the tower which the primitive fathers of mankind began to build.
The Greek form of Babel. The capital city of Babylonia and the seat of the Babylonian empire.
Devourer. The son of Beor, a prophet of Pethor in Mesopotamia.
A Greek word, consisting of two parts: baptizo, to dip, immerse, or wash; and the termination -ismos, denoting the act.
Son of consolation. A Levite of Cyprus, a companion of Paul.
Son of Tolmai. One of the twelve apostles.
Daughter of the oath. The wife of Uriah, afterward of David.
This word is used of flocks or herds of cattle (Exodus 22:5; Numbers 20:4).
The blessings pronounced by Christ at the opening of the Sermon on the Mount.
Lord of flies. The name of a god worshipped at Ekron. Used as a name for Satan.
Well of the oath. A city in the southern border of Palestine.
Bel protect the king. The last king of Babylon before the Persian conquest.
Son of my right hand. The youngest son of Jacob by Rachel.
House of dates. A village on the southeastern slope of the Mount of Olives.
House of God. The name of a place where Jacob had his vision of the ladder.
House of bread. A city in the hill country of Judah, the birthplace of Jesus.
The privilege of the first-born son. Included a double portion of the inheritance.
In the sense of speaking evil of God, this word does not occur in the Old Testament.
The giving of good; the bestowment of benefits; in the Old Testament, usually associated with the first-born.
The life of all flesh is in the blood. In the Mosaic economy, blood was the medium of atonement.
Alacrity. A wealthy Bethlehemite who married Ruth. Ancestor of David and Jesus.
The staff of life. Made from wheat, barley, or other grains.
The thorn bush from which God spoke to Moses on Horeb.
The surname of Julius Caesar, which became the official title of the Roman emperors.
A city on the Mediterranean coast of Palestine, built by Herod the Great.
A possession; a spear. The first-born son of Adam and Eve, who killed his brother Abel.
A dog. One of the spies sent to Canaan. He alone with Joshua urged the people to go up and possess the land.
Reeds. The scene of Christ's first miracle.
The son of Ham, and the country which bears his name.
The descendants of Canaan, the son of Ham. They occupied the land of Palestine before the Israelite conquest.
Village of Nahum. A city on the western shore of the Sea of Galilee.
Fortress of Chemosh. A city on the Euphrates, where Nebuchadnezzar defeated Pharaoh-necho.
A park; fruitful. A mountain range in Palestine, famous for Elijah's contest with the prophets of Baal.
An artificer in stone, iron, and copper, as well as in wood. Jesus was a carpenter.
Famous trees of the Lebanon mountain range, much used in building the Temple.
A Roman officer commanding 100 soldiers.
Plural: cherubim. Angelic beings, represented as winged creatures, often depicted as guardians.
The words of the days. The name of two books of the Old Testament containing genealogies and history.
In the New Testament, the assembly of believers in Christ. The called-out ones.
Cutting round. A rite instituted by God as a sign of the covenant with Abraham.
Six cities designated as places where persons guilty of manslaughter might find refuge.
The distinction between animals that might and might not be eaten, established in the Mosaic law.
The Holy Spirit, the Advocate, whom the Father would send in the name of Christ.
The Ten Words. The fundamental laws given to Moses on Mount Sinai.
An alphabetical index of the principal words in a book, with references to the passages in which they occur.
Consciousness of the moral right or wrong of one's own acts or motives.
A city of Greece, the capital of the Roman province of Achaia, where Paul established a church.
A centurion of the Italian cohort, the first Gentile convert to Christianity.
A contract or agreement between two parties. In the Old Testament, God's solemn agreement with His people.
The act of bringing the universe into existence by the word of God.
A formal statement of religious belief. The Apostles' Creed and Nicene Creed are the most famous.
A mode of punishment by fastening to a cross. The method of Christ's execution.
The sun. The founder of the Persian empire and conqueror of Babylon.
